coredata.dc:title: Polyethyleneimine-functionalized carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for doxorubicin hydrochloride by an inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Min
Yan, Yujia
Liu, Enzhou
Hu, Xiaoyun
Hao, Hong
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescence sensor was designed for the determination of doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) based on polyethyleneimine-functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CDs). The PEI-CDs were simply prepared using citric acid as a carbon source and PEI as a modifier based on the “synthesis-modification integration” method. The prepared PEI-CDs exhibited excellent optical properties, and the fluorescence emission was efficiency quenched in the presence of DOX based on inner filter effect (IFE). There was a linear relationship between quenching efficiency and DOX concentration in the range of 0.1–150 μM under the best detection conditions, and the detection limit as low as 75.2 nM. In addition, the new IFE-based sensing platform was successfully used to detect DOX in real samples with satisfactory results obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polyethyleneimine
Doxorubicin hydrochloride
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of DOX
Determination of DOX in urine samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276 ), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M600809 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of PEI-CDs. Inset: the particle size distribution of PEI-CDs.
The XRD spectrum (a) and FT-IR spectrum (b) of PEI-CDs.
Full-scale XPS spectra (a), C1s spectrum (b) and N1s spectrum (c) of PEI-CDs.
The PL spectra of PEI-CDs with different excitation wavelength (a) and different concentration (b).

The PL spectrum of PEI-CDs (blue) and the absorption spectrum of DOX (red) (a), and the Zeta potentials of PEI-CDs and DOX (b). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The PL spectra of 0.667 g/L PEI-CDs with different concentrations of DOX (a) and the linear plot of lnF and the concentration of DOX (b).

The fluorescence response of the PEI-CDs to DOX and other interfering substances including biomolecules (a) and ions (b). The concentration of DOX is 100 μM and the concentration of the other interfering substances is 10-fold of DOX.
The cytotoxicity of PEI-CDs on HeLa cells.

head.ce:title: Polyethyleneimine-functionalized carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for doxorubicin hydrochloride by an inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescence sensor was designed for the determination of doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) based on polyethyleneimine-functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CDs). The PEI-CDs were simply prepared using citric acid as a carbon source and PEI as a modifier based on the “synthesis-modification integration” method. The prepared PEI-CDs exhibited excellent optical properties, and the fluorescence emission was efficiency quenched in the presence of DOX based on inner filter effect (IFE). There was a linear relationship between quenching efficiency and DOX concentration in the range of 0.1–150 μM under the best detection conditions, and the detection limit as low as 75.2 nM. In addition, the new IFE-based sensing platform was successfully used to detect DOX in real samples with satisfactory results obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxorubincin hydrochloride (DOX) is an anthraquinone-based anti-tumor drug, which is widely used in clinical medicine to treat breast cancer, lymphoma, hematological malignancies, lung cancer and other tumor cells [1]. The imbalance of DOX concentration in biological fluids can cause many diseases, including nausea, hair loss, liver damage, vomiting, abdominal pain and heart failure. Therefore, real-time monitoring of DOX concentration is necessary [2]. At present, many efforts are devoted to the development of analytical techniques for the detection of DOX, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3,4], electrochemical methods [5,6], mass spectrometry [7], chemiluminescenc [8] and capillary electrochromatography [9]. Among the diversiform analytical means available, fluorescence spectroscopy has the advantages of facile operation procedures, high sensitivity and fast responses over other methods [10,11]. Nevertheless, it is still a challenging task to design a fluorescent sensor with a perfect combination of low-cost, easy-to-synthesize, high-stability and selectivity to determine DOX [12,13].

As the newest member of nanomaterials, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) with a size less than 10 nm have attracted widespread attention in recent years due to their unique optical and electrochemical properties [14]. Compared with traditional organic fluorescence dyes, CDs have the advantages of excellent photo/chemical-stability, good biocompatibility, robust chemical inertness and low toxicity, which makes possible many new techniques for bioanalysis and bioimaging fileds [15,16]. More importantly, its surface can be easily incorporated hydrophilic functional groups through various surface modifications such as ethylenediamine, polyethylene glycol (PEG) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) to broaden its application range [17,18]. At present, CDs have been applied in the determination of molecular substances, ions and pH [19–24]. The sensing mechanisms of CDs-based fluorescent sensor incorporate fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT), photo induced electron transfer (PET) and inner filter effect (IFE) etc [25]. In 1991, Gabor and Walt reported IFE for the first time, involving the participation of two chemical moieties, in which excitation light and/or emission light are absorbed by an absorber [26]. In the detection system, the linking between the absorbers and the fluorophores is no longer required, making the IFE-based fluorescent probe relatively simple and flexible [27]. Using IFE can convert the absorption value of the nanoprobe into a fluorescent signal, constructing a simple sensing method, which effectively avoids the complicated modification or construction of the nanoprobe and improves the sensitivity and selectivity of sensing. Hence, it has appeared as an effective strategy for the construction of fresh sensor platforms [28–31].

In this study, we successfully demonstrated a simple “synthesis-modification integration” means for the fabrication of PEI-functionalized CDs (PEI-CDs), shunning uncontrollable and intricate surface modification. By exploiting the complementary overlap between the emission band of PEI-CDs and the absorption band of DOX, an IFE-based DOX fluorescence sensor was constructed, which is absolutely different from the previously reported DOX assays. Such sensing technology would greatly decrease the testing cost, simplify testing procedure and exhibit high photostability. To evaluate the reliability of the sensing strategy, we successfully applied it to detect the concentration of DOX in urine samples and satisfactory results were obtained. At present, there are no reports about PEI-CDs as fluorophores to detect DOX based on IFE.
The relevant chemicals and materials used are listed in the Supplementary Materials, all reagents are used directly without further purification.

Fluorescence performances were performed using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer Record (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a quartz cell (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm) and a 150 W Xenon light source. The Zeta potentials were measured using a Nano-ZS analyzer (Brookhaven Instruments Ltd, US). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were characterized by a Rigaku D/MAX 3C powder diffract meter. The size and structure of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs were obtained by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Hitachi H-800 electron microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV with a CCD camera). UV–vis absorption spectra were taken by a Shimadzu UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) absorption spectra were carried out by PerkinElmer Frontier FT-IR spectrometer in the mid IR range with ATR accessory. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained using Kratos AXIS NOVA spectrometer.

PEI-CDs were prepared by one-step “synthesis-modification integration” method according to a referencing method [32]. Typically, 0.5 g PEI and 1.0 g CA were dispersed evenly in 10 mL deionized water, and the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, the as-obtained solution was purified by dialyzing against 150 mL ultrapure water for 15 h to remove unreacted impurities after the autoclave naturally cooling to room temperature. Finally, the obtained water-soluble PEI-CDs were stored at 4 °C for later use.

The quantum yield (QY) of PEI-CDs was calculated by the following equation using quinine sulfate as standard (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent, QY = 54%) [33,34]:
(1)
Q x
=
Q r
(
n x
2
n r
2
)
(
A r
A x
)
(
F x
F r
)

Here, Q, n, A and F represent quantum yield, refractive index of the solvent, absorption value at the excitation wavelength and fluorescence peak area, the subscripts x and r represents the determined and standard, respectively.

DOX aqueous solutions with different concentrations were freshly prepared before testing. The following procedure for determining DOX was implemented: 1.0 mL of stock solution of PEI-CDs, 1.0 mL of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution (pH = 7.24, 0.04 M) and 1.0 mL of DOX with different concentration were added to a 10 mL standard colorimetric plug tube, and the volume was calibrated to the scale line using deionized water. After incubating for 1 min, the fluorescence spectrum of the mixture was immediately measured with an excitation wavelength of 385 nm and the napierian logarithm of the fluorescence intensity value at 475 nm was calculated as the response signal.

Human urine samples were collected from grown-up volunteer (Local in Xi'an City, China) and stored in the refrigerator at −4 °C for later use. In order to obtain a real urine sample containing DOX, a DOX solution of unknown concentration was added to the urine before processing. Then, the mixture was centrifuged for 20 min at 10000 rpm and filtered with 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane to remove some possible particulate suspension solids. Then the obtained supernatant was diluted 100-fold using deionized water and used for analyzing.

The cytotoxicity assay of PEI-CDs was carried out by Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) method. Briefly, HeLa cells were grown in MEM medium (Corning, Art. No. 10-010-CV) supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS, Biological Industries, Art. No. 04-007-1A) and maintained at 37 °C in an incubator containing 5% CO2 (WIGGENSWCI-180). The HeLa cells were seed in 96-well plates at a concentration of 4 × 103 cells/well and treated in the presence of PEI-CDs with different concentrations (0.133–1 mg/mL) for 24 h. After incubation, 20 μL CCK-8 (Invigentech, Art.No. IV08-100) solution was added to each well followed by 2 h incubation at 37 °C, and the absorbance at 450 nm was recorded by using a microplate Multiskan Spectrum (TECAN, SPARK 10 M). Fresh culture medium was used as control group, and each sample was repeated three times. The Relative Cell Viability of HeLa cell was calculated as follows:
(2) Relative Cell Viability=(ATest-ABackground)/(AControl-ABackground) × 100%
Where ATest, AControl and ABackground present the absorbance value of control group (no PEI-CDs solution added), test group and porous plate itself (without medium and cell).

In earlier research, CDs are normally defined as small carbons nanoparticles with all kinds of surface passivation forms, among which the surface functionalization or modification with polymeride or organic molecules is believed to be effective for brightening fluorescence emissions [35]. Although the exhaustive mechanism of carbon dots formation is still unclear, it could be deduced that CA is first decomposed to produce soluble molecules in a high temperature environment, then these soluble molecules undergone polymerization and condensation, and the CDs were finally formed by carbonization [36]. Fig. 1 summarizes the synthesis process of the PEI-CDs. The preparation of the PEI-CDs was carried out through one-step hydrothermal treatment of a mixture of PEI and CA (1:2 wt ratio). CA, as a tricarboxylic acid compound, has been generally used as a carbon source in the synthesis to obtain uniform CDs nanoparticles. In order to further improve the fluorescence performance of CDs and get more binding sites to identify the analyte on its surface, PEI with poliamine structure was selected as a functionalizing regent to achieve the amidation reaction between the amino group of PEI and the carboxyl group of CDs.

The morphological structure and size of PEI-CDs were characterized and analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The prepared PEI-CDs are spherical particles with well dispersibility (as shown in Fig. 2 a), according to the statistics of particle size distribution (inset of Fig. 2a), the average diameter of PEI-CDs is about 15.94 nm. In addition, the average diameter of CDs is about 3.2 nm (Fig. S1c), where CDs are obtained by treating citric acid under the same conditions. These results indicate that the increase of particle size of PEI-CDs is due to the modification of PEI. Furthermore, the HRTEM image reveals that the PEI-CDs have obvious lattice planes with interplanar d-spacing of 0.39 nm in Fig. 2b, corresponding to the (120) planes of disordered carbons.

XRD was applied to further investigate the crystalline structure and purity of the PEI-CDs. Fig. 3 a shows the XRD pattern of PEI-CDs, and an obvious diffraction peak can be observed at around 23°, corresponding to the (120) plane of highly disordered PEI-CDs (JCPDS No. 50–0926). It is consistent with the results of TEM analysis. The FT-IR absorption spectrum analysis results of PEI-CDs are showed in Fig. 3b. The broad peaks around 3474 and 1637 cm−1 of PEI-CDs are assigned to the stretching and bending vibration modes of the N–H bond. The characteristic peak near 1549 cm−1 corresponds to the C=ONR bond, indicating that PEI could interact with the carboxyl group of carbon dots by amidation reaction to achieve surface modification and provide positive charge for the surface of carbon dots [18]. The peak at 2953 cm−1 is considered to be the C–H band stretching vibrations. The speaks at 1297 and 3299 cm−1 represent the bending and stretching vibration of –OH, respectively. The characteristic peak at 1403 cm−1 is corresponded to stretching vibrations of C–N, and the bending vibration of C–O emerges at 1034 cm−1. The peaks at 1180 and 1697 cm−1 represent C–O–C and CO stretching vibration, respectively, showing that there are multiple oxygen-containing groups on the surface of PEI-CDs during the synthesis process. Compared with the FTIR spectra of PEI and CDs (Fig. S1a), the FTIR spectrum of PEI-CDs show the N–H bond characteristic peaks of PEI, and the appearance of amide bond characteristic peak indicates the interaction between PEI and CDs. These characteristic peaks do not exist in the FTIR spectrum of CDs, which confirmed the successful modification of CDs by PEI.

The chemical states of the as-prepared PEI-CDs were studied by XPS. Fig. 4 a reveals the full-rang spectrum with three remarkable peaks at 284.8, 399.8 and 531.8 eV attributing to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [37]. Fig. 4b illustrates the C1s peaks corresponding to CC/C–C (284.3 eV), C–N (284.9 eV), C–O (285.6 eV) and CO (288.1 eV) [38]. The presence of CC/C–C is considered to be the main component of the PEI-CDs skeleton and the existence of CO structure combined with baring hydroxyl or C–O–C indicates intermolecular dehydration happened during the synthesis of PEI-CDs [8]. The N1s region can be deconvolved into two peaks relating to C–N (398.9 eV) and N–H (400.2 eV) [39] (as shown in Fig. 4c). O is mainly combined with C, the O1s peak will not be decomposed in detail here.

The optical properties of PEI-CDs were researched by recording UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of PEI-CDs (Fig. 5 a) exhibits a typical absorption peak at 356 nm belonging to n→п* transition of CO [40]. Moreover, the fluorescence emission spectra of PEI-CDs were measured to further characterize the emission performance. It is widely perceived that most carbon dots have excitation/concentration-dependent fluorescence properties [41,42]. Under different excitation wavelengths, the fluorescence emission peaks of PEI-CDs show a gradual red shift as the excitation wavelength increases from 390 to 510 nm with an interval of 20 nm in Fig. 6 a, which is mainly ascribed to the difference in the distribution of the emission traps and the different excited surface states at different excitation wavelengths. In addition, the emission peaks of PEI-CDs successively move towards large wavelength and the fluorescence intensity gradually descends with the increase of PEI-CDs concentration (Fig. 6b), illustrating PEI-CDs possess concentration-dependent fluorescence performance, which can be derived from the self-quenching effect of PEI-CDs as the concentration increase. In addition, the fluorescence emission peak of PEI-CDs is significantly red-shifted compared to CDs (Fig. S1b), which can prove that the size of PEI-CDs is larger than that of CDs.

The fluorescence QYs of PEI-CDs was calculated as 22.88% with quinine sulfate as reference. Most importantly, it is very necessary to investigate the photostability of PEI-CDs because the excitation light required for QDs is very susceptible to destroy its surface structure [43]. The fluorescence intensity values at the maximum emission wavelength of PEI-CDs under 360 nm ultraviolet light irradiation at different times were recorded. The results of the investigation showed that the fluorescence intensities of PEI-CDs did not change obviously with the prolongation of radiation time (Fig. S2), which meaning that PEI-CDs have excellent resistance to photobleaching capacity.

The IFE usually describes as an absorption of the fluorophore emission/excitation light by absorber in the sensing system [44,45]. Furthermore, it has been displayed that an excellent spectral overlap between the emission/excitation band of the fluorophore and the absorption band of the absorber will bring a high efficiency IFE [46,47]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of DOX and fluorescence emission spectrum of PEI-CDs were recorded. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, there is a good overlap between the wide absorption band of DOX and the emission band of PEI-CDs (in the yellow areas). Therefore, upon addition of DOX, the emitted light of PEI-CDs can be effectively absorbed by DOX, resulting in fluorescence intensity based on the IFE. At the same time, Zeta potential measurement results display that DOX (+42.7ev) and PEI-CDs (+33.5ev) have the same charge (Fig. 7a), so there is a Coulomb repulsion between them, which increases the distance between them. Therefore, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) cannot occur between DOX and PEI-CDs. In addition, the average lifetime times of PEI-CDs in the absence and presence of DOX are 12.5 and 12.1 ns, respectively (Fig. S3), which is consistent with the constant fluorescence lifetime during the IFE.

The sensitivity of this sensing method in large measure depends on the relevant experimental parameters such as concentration of PEI-CDs, pH values of solution and response time. Firstly, the effect of PEI-CDs concentration was discussed. The linear models are established based on different concentrations of PEI-CDs (0.533, 0.667 and 1.000 g/L, the concentration of PEI-CDs was calculated based on the feeding amount of CA). It can be seen from Figs. S4 and S5 that there is a low linear correlation between the fluorescence response signal (lnF) and DOX concentration when the concentration of PEI-CDs was high or low, where F is the fluorescent intensity of PEI-CDs at 475 nm. And there is a good linear relationship between lnF and DOX concentration when the concentration of PEI-CDs was 0.667 g/L. Therefore, 0.667 g/L is selected as the optimal concentration. Moreover, the effect of pH value on the detection results was investigated by adding Britton Robinson (BR) buffer solution with different pH values to the system. In Fig. S4a, the highest fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs and comparable fluorescence quenching efficiency are obtained at pH 7.24. Thus, 7.24 is chosen as the optimum pH value in this work. In fact, the fluctuation of PEI-CDs fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission wavelength is not obvious at different pH values, which further indicates that PEI-CDs has strong stability. Finally, upon the addition of 100 μM DOX into the solution of PEI-CDs, a speedy equilibration for the reaction can be obtained in 1 min (Fig. S4b). On the basis of these investigations, different concentrations of DOX were incubated with 0.667 g/L PEI-CDs at pH 7.24 for 1 min before fluorescence testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of DOX
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the proposed sensing method was evaluated by recording the fluorescence intensity changes of PEI-CDs with different concentrations of DOX. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a, the fluorescence emission of PEI-CDs is effectively inhibited with the increase of DOX concentrations. Fig. 8b displays a good linear relationship between the quenching efficiency (lnF) and DOX concentration in the range of 0.1–150 μM, and the detection limit was calculated as 75.2 nM based on S/N = 3. The linear equation for the resulting calibration plot is lnF = 8.535–0.0132C with linear correlation coefficient of 0.992 (F is the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs at 475 nm, C is the concentration of DOX), proving that this method was highly dependable.

Selectivity is another significant factor in assessing the performance of new sensor. To estimate the response specificity of the developed method, the interference of some potential competitors at 1 mM (Glu, AA, Cys, Na+, Ca2+, Cl−, and so on) was tested under the same conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 9 a and b, most interfering substances have a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system when the concentrations of these potentially competing substances is 10 times that of DOX. As a consequence, the above results clearly illustrate that the designed sensing method can be used for selective and quantitative detection of DOX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DOX in urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the feasibility of the IFE-based sensing method, it was used for the determination of DOX in real urine samples. Recovery tests were performed by spiking the urine samples with various amounts of standard DOX, the measurement results are summarized in Table S1 and the recovery rates obtained range from 99.98% to 100.25%. The above results illustrate the accuracy and reliability of the developed IFE-based assay method for the detection of DOX in real samples.

Up to now, many novel sensing methods have been constructed and employed to DOX determination. However, the sensitivity is still a challenge. Furthermore, we compared our assay method with other analytical methods that have previously been reported for determining DOX in Table S2 [4,48–51]. While the detection limit is comparable to other DOX sensors, and the wide linear range makes this sensor rather appealing. Generally known, different sensing methods have different preponderances and disadvantages. Compared with other sensors, the IFE-based fluorescence analysis approaches showed a series of attractive superiorities including no complex preseparation procedure, cost-effective, rapidity and reliability.

HeLa cells were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of PEI-CDs. It can be displayed that the relative cell viability of HeLa cells after incubation 24 h with different concentrations PEI-CDs (0.133, 0.266, 0.399, 0.532, 0.667, 1 mg/mL) in Fig. 10 . The assay results show that the relative cell viability does not decrease significantly with the increase of PEI-CDs concentration, which illustrates that the PEI-CDs have no cytotoxicity and possess promising application prospects in practical sample detection.

In a word, we have systematically studied the detection mechanism for the quenching of PEI-CDs by DOX, and established a new IFE-based system for sensing DOX. The developed detection method was applied to the determination of DOX in urine samples, and satisfactory results were gained. The most remarkable advantage of this assay method is that it can avoid time-consuming and complex chemical modifications. Therefore, we believe that the developed IFE-based fluorescence sensing means will provide a novel opportunity for the development and design of facile and effective sensors through the use of PEI-CDs as probes.

Min Yang: Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Data processing, Manuscript writing. Yujia Yan: Material preparation and characterization. Enzhou Liu: Funding acquisition. Xiaoyun Hu: Funding acquisition. Hong Hao: Manuscript correction. Jun Fan: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M600809).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2020.110743.
